resume:
Dr. Marcus Hale, MD, MBA — Director of Clinical Operations

Mercy Valley Health System (2019–present)
Director, Clinical Operations — 3 hospitals, 1,400 beds, 11 service lines.
- Cut average ED length-of-stay from 6.4h to 4.1h over 18 months.
- Led sepsis-bundle compliance program; mortality down 19%.
- Own a $140M operating budget; 7 direct reports.

Northside Regional (2014–2019)
Associate Medical Director, Inpatient Services.
- Stood up a hospitalist staffing model across 4 units.

Education: MD, State Medical School; MBA, healthcare track.
Board-certified, Internal Medicine.
Licensure: active, two states.
jd: We are recruiting a Director of Clinical Operations to oversee throughput, quality, and staffing across a three-hospital acute-care network. This role owns length-of-stay reduction, core-measure compliance (sepsis, stroke, AMI bundles), and a multi-disciplinary capacity-command function. You will partner with the CMO and CNO, manage a nine-figure operating budget, and be accountable to the board quality committee for measurable mortality and throughput outcomes. We expect candidates to speak precisely about specific interventions, the metric movement they drove, and the timeframe — vague leadership narratives will not pass this panel.

history.q: You report cutting ED length-of-stay from 6.4 to 4.1 hours. Tell me the single intervention that moved the needle the most, and over what period.

candidate_last_answer: The biggest lever was a physician-in-triage model in the ED front end. We put an attending at intake from 11am to 11pm, the two peak-arrival shifts. That alone took door-to-disposition from 142 minutes to 96 minutes within the first quarter. The remaining gain came from a discharge-by-11am bed protocol, which freed inpatient beds earlier and cut boarding. Net LOS went 6.4 to 5.0 in two quarters, then 5.0 to 4.1 over the following year as we tightened the discharge protocol.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: We put an attending at intake from 11am to 11pm

expected-yield: whether the candidate personally drove the physician-in-triage staffing change — who he had to convince (CMO, ED chair, the attendings whose schedules changed), how he funded the added coverage, and what tradeoff he accepted to staff those peak shifts
